title: The Allocation of Socially Responsible Capital
abstract: Portfolio allocation decisions increasingly incorporate social values. We develop a tractable framework to study how competition between investors to own socially valuable assets affects social welfare. Relative to the most common social-investing strategies, we identify alternative strategies that result in higher impact and higher financial returns. We identify strategies for investors to have impact when impact is difficult to measure. From the firm's perspective, increasing profitability can have greater impact than directly increasing social value. We present new empirical evidence on the social preferences of investors that demonstrates the practical relevance of our theory.

article.introduction:
The last decade has seenan invigoration of investing in companies that rank favorably on metrics of social value, such as environmental stewardship, social responsibility, and good governance practices (collectively referred to as ESG). This shift in investment strategies has the potential to alter the allocation of capital in the economy. Indeed, many argue that reallocating resources from socially harmful uses to socially beneficial uses is the point of the ESG investing. It is therefore important to understand whether this style of investing achieves its intended effect, and if so, how. This paper develops a theoretical framework to explore how socially motivated investing creates social value.
We focus on strategies based on portfolio composition rather than engagement. The most common of such strategies in practice are based on the financial returns and social value of the companies included in an investor's portfolio. For example, ESG index funds attempt to track the returns of a benchmark index while maximizing some composite measure of the social good of the companies in the portfolio. Proponents of such “values-aligned” investing claim that they increase the valuation of (or equivalently decrease the cost of capital for) economic activities that contribute the most positively to society. This in turn shifts the set of projects that markets finance away from those that destroy social value and toward those that create it.
We argue that the conventional wisdom justifying values-aligned investing is misguided, and that such investment strategies are an inefficient way to use asset allocation to create social value. As the prevalence of socially motivated investing grows, investors invariably compete for the right to invest in, or own securities issued by, more socially valuable firms. We show that this competition can limit investor impact and make it more expensive.
In this example, the social investors fail to generate social value. In their absence, the equilibrium allocation of capital would be unchanged, as FirmsA$A$andB$B$could be commercially financed. Competition between commercial and social investors displaces commercial investors and thus limits social investor impact. Further, competition between the social investors lowers the returns to the social investor in FirmB$B$even though this investor has no social impact.
Now imagine that the investor financing FirmB$B$were to deviate and instead finance FirmC$C$. Facing no competition from other social or commercial investors, this investor could earn a return of4%$4\%$, an improvement over the return they received financing FirmB$B$. Moreover, since investing in FirmC$C$does not displace a commercial investor, all three firms would be funded and total social value would increase. Thus, there exists a deviation from a values-aligned investing strategy that increases not only impact but also financial returns.
While this example is stark and highly stylized, we show these forces persist in a more general model in which many commercial investors and social investors compete and firms' costs of capital is determined in equilibrium. To highlight the limits of typical socially motivated investment strategies in the presence of competing investors, our framework encompasses two types of social investors.Values-alignedsocial investors, as in the example and consistent with observed real-world ESG investing strategies, form portfolios as if they care only about their financial returns and the social value generated by the firms in which they invest.Impact-alignedsocial investors, in contrast, care about their financial returns and the consequences of their investment decisions for total social welfare.
Beyond admitting a tractable analysis of equilibrium behavior, our model yields several positive and normative implications for social investors and entrepreneurs. First, we show that in equilibrium, values-aligned social investors and entrepreneurs exhibit positive assortative matching—investors who care more about social welfare match to entrepreneurs that create more social value. More interestingly, when social investors are impact-aligned, a form of negative assortative matching emerges whereby the investors who care more about social welfare match to entrepreneurs who create less social value. This can be viewed as an extension of the argument that impact-aligned investors do not want to displace investors with less concern for social value, whether that be commercial investors or other social investors with a lower degree of altruism.
Second, as the example highlights, we identify improvements to the equilibrium investment strategies of values-aligned social investors that create more social impactanddeliver higher financial returns. When investment opportunities with high social value are also profitable enough to attract commercial investment, values-aligned investors compete with and displace commercial investors that would have otherwise financed these activities. Further, competition between social investors leads to a financial concession that is wasteful from the perspective of generating impact. We show that part of the price impact generated by values-aligned investment results in a transfer from investors to existing firm owners, without expanding the pool of socially valuable projects. This generates scope to increase not only the impact of values-aligned investment strategies, but also the financial return.
Third, we use our model to speak to an important issue in impact investing, the measurement of impact itself. In particular, we ask how impact-aligned strategies are affected by informational frictions that limit agents' ability to assess social investment opportunities. We extend our baseline model to incorporate incomplete information about both the profitability and the social value of investment opportunities. We show that incomplete information about profits, not just about social value, limits the impact that impact-aligned investors are able to achieve. It also leads impact-aligned investors to adopt strategies that look similar to those of values-aligned investors: They are willing to fund projects that appear commercially viable in stark contrast to optimal impact-aligned investment strategies in the complete-information model.
How important are our findings in practice? The answer depends on the extent to which socially minded investors adopt values-aligned strategies, as well as on the extent to which they do so to operationalize a true preference for impact alignment. Our results suggest that this could happen because (i) investors mistakenly adopt values-aligned strategies and (ii) informational frictions make such strategies optimal for impact-aligned investors. To shed light on how these investors operationalize these preferences, we conduct a laboratory experiment that elicits investors' social preferences. We find significant heterogeneity in social investing preferences, with sizable groups of participants caring only about their impact, others caring only about values alignment, and yet others having no willingness to trade off financial returns for either impact or values alignment. Moreover, even in our experimental environment without informational frictions, we find that some investors with impact-aligned preferences mistakenly adopt values-aligned investment strategies. These findings together suggest that the prevalence of values-aligned investing is due at least in part to mistaken adoption of values-aligned strategies by investors who have a preference for impact creation.
Our framework also has implications for evaluating the social impact of a firm, its enterprise impact (Brest, Gilson, and Wolfson (2019)). Enterprise impact not only depends on the amount of capital, but also thetypeof capital, used by the enterprise. All else equal, enterprises that attract the capital of socially minded investors contribute less to social welfare than do those that only attract the capital of commercial investors. Holding fixed the social value created by a firm, it can raise its enterprise impact by reducing its dependence on social capital, freeing social capital to fund an enterprise that is unable to obtain commercial financing. The more profitable a firm, the less likely it is to rely on scarce, socially valuable capital. Our framework therefore provides a new connection between the profitability of an enterprise and its contribution to social welfare.
This paper contributes to a growing literature on investing with social preferences, and in particular on whether these preferences can lead to more socially desirable economic activity. The majority of this literature studies investors who value investment in socially responsible activities, rather than investor preferences for generating impact through their investment strategies. A key theme of this literature is that the high degree of substitutability of capital limits the ability of socially motivated investors to have impact. In standard equilibrium asset pricing models, the extent to which a tilt toward more socially desirable investments can affect their relative cost of capital depends on the degree to which such tilt limits aggregate risk sharing in the economy (Heinkel, Kraus, and Zechner (2001), Pastor, Stambaugh, and Taylor (2021), Pedersen, Fitzgibbons, and Pomorski (2021), Zerbib (2022), Berk and Van Binsbergen (2024)).
Building on this work, we highlight a new mechanism through which impact of values-aligned investment strategies is limited. Consistent with the above literature, we find that values-aligned investment strategies can in principle shift capital to more socially valuable activities. However, we further show that these strategies are inefficient at generating impact in terms of the return concession required because competition between social investors to hold socially responsible investments bids prices up more than necessary for an investor to achieve a given social impact.
This paper also connects closely to a smaller theoretical literature asking how social investors should behave to maximize their impact. Broccardo, Hart, and Zingales (2022) and Edmans, Levit, and Schneemeier (2022) argue that engagement and “tilting” strategies, respectively, are more effective than divestment in generating impact. Oehmke and Opp (2024) and Landier and Lovo (2023) study activist social investors who aim to induce firms to adopt green investment choices in the presence of managerial moral hazard. In contrast to these papers, we study a complete information environment in which passive investors seek to facilitate new socially valuable projects by offering cheaper capital to firms with such projects. In their setting, Oehmke and Opp (2024) show that values-aligned social investors cannot generate social impact because they would rather not invest than invest in an improved but still polluting company. Our results, in contrast, stem from the role of competition between investors to hold socially valuable investments, which is not modeled in their paper.1
Our empirical evidence contributes to the literature that estimates the social preferences of investors. Heeb et al. (2023) find that where investors have a preference for impact, willingness to pay for impact does not scale with its magnitude. More closely related to our empirical exercise, Bonnefon et al. (2023) conduct an experiment to distinguish between preferences for values alignment and impact alignment, and find that the representative social investor has at best a modest preference for impact. Humphrey et al. (2021) and Nofsinger, Sulaeman, and Varma (2019) find that investors are more attentive to negative social attributes than positive social attributes. We make two important contributions to this literature. First, we identify heterogeneity in investors' social preferences. In particular, we show that a sizable share of investor types appear to have strong preferences for impact alignment. Second, we show that investors may be incorrectly operationalizing their preferences in their social investing strategies.
article.I. Baseline Model:
There is a finite setE$E$of entrepreneurs. Each entrepreneur is endowed with a project that requires one unit of capital. If entrepreneuri$i$receives the requisite capital, their project returnsπi∈R+$\pi _{i}\in \mathbb {R}^{+}$in profit andwi∈R$w_{i}\in \mathbb {R}$in “social value,” whereπi$\pi _{i}$andwi$w_{i}$represent the private and social returns of the project, respectively.2We assume that the features of each project are perfectly observable to all players.
There is a finite setS$S$of social investors, each of whom allocates one unit of capital.3There is also a market for commercial capital that elastically supplies financing to all firms at required rate of returnrC$r^{C}$.
A contract between an investor and entrepreneuri$i$specifies the transfer of one unit of capital from the investor to the entrepreneur in exchange for financial returnri$r_{i}$on their invested capital. The entrepreneur receives a share of profits,πi−ri$\pi _{i}-r_{i}$, andwi$w_{i}$social value is created. We sometimes refer tori$r_{i}$as the price or cost of capital offered to an entrepreneur. Because we are studying a complete-information environment without contracting frictions, this contract can be understood as either debt or equity. In addition to being able to finance the entrepreneurs inE$E$, social investors can allocate their capital to a social value neutral asset with financial returnrC$r^{C}$and zero social value.
We index investors and entrepreneurs such that investori$i$matches with entrepreneuri$i$. Each entrepreneur's utility is their share of the profit,πi−ri$\pi _{i}-r_{i}$. We separately examine two classes of social investors.
There are two interpretations of the assumption that values-aligned investors do not fully internalize the implications of their investment decision on social welfare. Under the first interpretation, values-aligned investors may derive intrinsic utility from owning firms that create social value, similar to the warm-glow altruists in Andreoni (1990). In this case, the analysis to follow can be understood as exploring the positive implications of these two modes of investment behavior. Under the second interpretation, values-aligned preferences represent thebehaviorof socially conscious investors, while impact-aligned social preferences more closely represent theintentionsof socially conscious investors to affect social change. In this case, our analysis of the behavior of impact-aligned social investors can be understood as offering normative guidance to real-world investors with social preferences. The empirical evidence, which we describe in SectionIV, suggests that both interpretations are relevant.
First, in theoffer stage, social investors offer contracts to entrepreneurs5and all firms receive an offer for commercial financing at raterC$r^{C}$. Next, in theacceptance stage, entrepreneurs choose at most one contract to accept and payoffs are realized.
The solution concept is pure-strategy subgame perfect equilibrium. In the acceptance stage, all entrepreneurs choose the contract offered to them that maximizes their share of the profits. In the offer stage, all investors choose the contract that maximizes their utility among all contracts that will be accepted, given the contracts offered by other investors.
Our measure of social welfare isW=∑i∈E¯wi$W=\underset{i\in \bar{E}}{\sum }w_{i}$, whereE¯$\bar{E}$is the set of entrepreneurs that receive financing. Our interpretation is thatwi$w_{i}$is the total social value created by firmi$i$if it receives financing, including the value to the firm's owners.6Impact-aligned social investors can therefore be understood to be maximizing a modified variant of social welfare that increases the weight placed on their own consumption. Note that, consistent with Hart and Zingales (2017) and Broccardo, Hart, and Zingales (2022), our measure of welfare does not include the “altruistic” utility that social investors derive from the creation of social valuewi$w_{i}$. The exclusion of investors' altruistic utility from the social welfare function can be motivated by assuming that the utility that investors derive from supporting socially valuable firms is “small” relative to the other ways in which such firms contribute to social value.
To present our results in a parsimonious manner, our model omits several important features of financial markets that are relevant for social investing. First, our model accommodates only a limited form of endogenous firm entry. In our model, the universe of potential firms is fixed. The allocation of commercial and social capital determines which of these firms ultimately enter the market, but entrepreneurs cannot respond by altering the distribution of potential firms. Second, because we assume that the commercial market elastically supplies capital at raterC$r^{C}$, rather than assume a finite supply of commercial capital, there is no channel for social investors to increase the cost of capital of firms with negative social value through divestment. In SectionI.Aof theInternet Appendix, we endogenize the commercial cost of capital and demonstrate that in this variant of the model, divestment has an impact, but allowing for this does not change our main results. Third, in our baseline model, entrepreneurs cannot endogenously change the properties of existing firms in response to market prices. In SectionI.Cof theInternet Appendix, we relax this constraint and again show that our main results are uneffected.
Finally, we note that our model features a finite number of investors, each with a discrete amount of capital. This modeling choice ensures that each investor is able to have a positive, measurable impact on social welfare and, in turn, is able to operationalize their preference for impact in their portfolio strategies. While this modeling choice maps well into private capital markets with large investors, it is still possible for smaller investors to have an impact. One way to interpret our model is to conceive of each investor as a fund, which aggregates the capital of smaller investors as in Landier and Lovo (2023). Alternatively, in SectionII.C.11of theInternet Appendix, we discuss how our model can be extended to a limiting case in which investors are atomistic but impact-aligned investors' regard for social impact is nonetheless preserved in the limit.
article.II. Analysis of Baseline Model:
To shed light on the behavior of values-aligned and impact-aligned social investors, we begin by separately characterizing the equilibrium of the model in which either all investors are values-aligned or all investors are impact-aligned. In SectionII.C, we present model results when both types of social investors exist.
Lemma 1.For any two values-aligned social investorsi$i$andj$j$(matched in equilibrium to entrepreneursi$i$andj$j$), ifθi≥θj$\theta _{i}\ge \theta _{j}$, thenwi≥wj$w_{i}\ge w_{j}$.
Lemma1is an implication of the fact that investors' preferences are supermodular inθi$\theta _{i}$andwi$w_{i}$. Social investors with relatively higher altruismθi$\theta _{i}$have relatively higher willingness to pay per unit of an entrepreneur's social value. Therefore, in equilibrium, investors with the highest altruism finance enterprises with the highest social value. This result is a point of contrast to the case in which investors are impact-aligned.
Our next observation, which again follows from the incentive compatibility condition above, is that cost of capital is decreasing in an entrepreneur's social valuewi$w_{i}$.
Lemma 2.For any two entrepreneurs financed in equilibrium by values-aligned social investors, ifwi>wj$w_{i}>w_{j}$, thenri≤rj$r_{i}\le r_{j}$. This inequality is strict whenri>0$r_{i}>0$andrj>0$r_{j}>0$.8

Lemma2plays an important part in the analysis to follow, and is another important point of contrast to the case in which social investors are impact-aligned. When investors are values-aligned, the fact that they compete with one another to be the owners of socially valuable companies leads them to bid up the prices of companies with higher social value. As we discuss below, in some cases, this expands the financing frontier for socially valuable companies, while in other cases, it simply increases the share of profits retained by entrepreneurs with socially valuable companies.
Figure1depicts the equilibrium matching of investors to entrepreneurs. The graph shows the space of available investment opportunities, parameterized byπ$\pi$andw$w$, and the shaded regions show the sets of entrepreneurs financed by different types of investors.
Entrepreneurs in the lightest blue region, with profits above the commercial hurdle raterC$r^{C}$but with relatively low social value, are financed by the commercial market. Entrepreneurs with progressively higher social valuewi$w_{i}$while financed by social investors with progressively higher levels of altruism, and entrepreneurs in the unshaded region are not financed in equilibrium. The financing frontier becomes progressively steeper as investors become more altruistic (with slope−1/θi$-1/\theta ^{i}$), reflecting their increased willingness to trade off financial returns for social value. SectionII.Aof theInternet Appendixdescribes the precise strategies that support this equilibrium.
This model abstracts from investors' risk-aversion and diversification motives. In the presence of such motives, while values-aligned investors would still finance projects with relatively higher social valueon average, they would also finance projects with low social value that improve diversification of the investor's portfolio (Pedersen, Fitzgibbons, and Pomorski (2021)).
We next analyze the equilibrium allocation of capital when all social investors are impact-aligned. One of the principal differences relative to the case in which social investors are values-aligned is that, so long as social capital is sufficiently scarce, impact-aligned investors do not finance commercially viable entrepreneurs regardless of their social value. We maintain the following assumption in the remainder of SectionII.B.
Assumption 1.Social capital is scarce:S≤i∈E:πi+θ1wi>rC,πi<rC$\left|S\right|\le \left|\left\lbrace i\in E:\pi _{i}+\theta ^{1}w_{i}>r^{C},\pi _{i}< \right.\right. \left.\left. r^{C}\right\rbrace \right|$.

Assumption1accounts for entrepreneurs that are not commercially viable (i.e.,πi<rC$\pi _{i}<r^{C}$), with projects whose profit plus social value as judged by the social investor with lowest altruism exceeds the commercial rate of return (i.e.,πi+θ1wi>rC$\pi _{i}+\theta ^{1}w_{i}>r^{C}$). If the number of such entrepreneurs is greater than the number of social investors (i.e., when Assumption1holds), then impact-aligned investors never finance entrepreneurs that could have attracted commercial capital.
Lemma 3.In equilibrium, no impact-aligned investor finances an entrepreneur withπi≥rC$\pi _{i}\ge r^{C}$.

When social capital is scarce, there is always a noncommercially viable investment opportunity that an impact-aligned investori$i$would prefer to financing a commercially viable investment. Financing a commercially viable investment delivers returns of at mostrC$r^{C}$. Because doing so displaces commercial capital, it generates no social value. In contrast, by Assumption1, there is always some unfinanced firmj$j$satisfyingπj+θiwj>rC$\pi _{j}+\theta _{i}w_{j}>r^{C}$.
Lemma3presents an important point of contrast between our baseline model, where firms have binary scale, and models in which firms have more degrees of freedom in their production functions. In our baseline model, social investors who finance a commercially viable firm do not have impact, because the firm would be financed regardless. In contrast, in Landier and Lovo (2023) and Oehmke and Opp (2024), investors can have impact by displacing commercial investors, because in doing so they can induce a firm to adopt clean, rather than dirty technology. In SectionI.Cof theInternet Appendix, we consider a model in which firms have continuous production functions and show that investors can have an impact by investing in commercially viable firms to induce them to change their scales of operation relative to if they were purely commercially financed.
We further note that the stark result in Lemma3arises in part because investors in our model have no diversification motive. In the presence of such motives, impact-aligned investors would be willing to invest in some commercially viable firms to reduce portfolio risk, even though doing so does not impact social welfare.
While Lemma3holds across all equilibria, the precise set of firms that receive financing from impact-aligned social investors is not fixed across all equilibria. Nevertheless, we have the following characterization of the capital allocation in the investor-optimal and welfare-optimal equilibrium, which reverses the positive assortative matching result for values-aligned investors.
Proposition 1.Consider the investor- and welfare-optimal equilibrium. For any two entrepreneursi$i$andj$j$that receive capital from impact-aligned social investors withπi=πj$\pi _{i}=\pi _{j}$, ifwi>wj$w_{i}>w_{j}$, thenθi≤θj$\theta _{i}\le \theta _{j}$.9

Proposition1implies that when social investors are impact-aligned, they exhibit a form of negative assortative matching in equilibrium. Among firms with a fixed level of profitsπ$\pi$, the higher is the social investor's altruism parameterθi$\theta _{i}$, the lower is the social valuewi$w_{i}$of the firm they support. This negative assortative matching holds despite the fact that the utility of impact-aligned social investors is supermodular in their altruism parameterθi$\theta _{i}$and the social valuewi$w_{i}$of the firm they support (as is the case with values-aligned social investors).
The investor- and welfare-optimal equilibrium allocation is depicted in Figure2. SectionII.Bof theInternet Appendixformally characterizes the allocation of impact-aligned social capital.
Before proceeding, we note that impact-aligned investors do not compete to fund assets by offering the best financing terms. This is because they recognize that undercutting another investor does not contribute to the creation of social welfare. This introduces the question of how exactly allocation of impact investors to investment opportunities occurs. In our model, Nash equilibrium ensures that no two investors attempt to finance the same firm. We view this as a stand-in for more realistic features of the economy that facilitate the matching of investors to projects, for example, informational and search frictions that create dispersion in beliefs about project quality and opportunities to finance them.
Related, in our baseline model, as a consequence of the fact that impact-aligned investors have no incentive to undercut one another, they extract the full profits of the firms they finance. In SectionI.Hof theInternet Appendix, we demonstrate that our main results—Propositions2and3—do not rely on the assumption that impact-aligned social investors extract the full profits of the firms they finance. In particular, we alter the model to allow entrepreneurs, rather than investors, to make take-it-or-leave-it offers, and in equilibrium entrepreneurs retain positive profits.
In the following sections, we discuss normative results about social investing in a market in which both impact-aligned and values-aligned social investors exist. Much of the analysis above continues to hold when both types of social investors are present in the same market. Lemmas1and2carry over to the case with both types of social investors. Values-aligned social investors and entrepreneurs continue to engage in positive assortative matching, and the cost of capital is decreasing in social valuew$w$for any two entrepreneurs financed by values-aligned social investors. Lemma3also carries over to the case with both types of social investors. So long as social capital is sufficiently scarce, impact-aligned social investors never finance an entrepreneur that could have attracted commercial capital.
A generic equilibrium allocation is depicted in Figure3.
In this section, we consider the following thought exercise. Holding the equilibrium behavior of all other investors fixed, we consider the possibility of reallocating the investment of a single values-aligned social investor. We demonstrate that any values-aligned social investor who supports a firm withπi≥rC$\pi _{i}\ge r^{C}$and who earns a financial return ofri<rC$r_{i}<r^{C}$could reallocate their capital to increase both total social welfare and their financial return. In this sense, values-aligned investors leave both money and impact on the table.
Proposition 2.Consider any values-aligned social investori$i$who supports a firm withπi>rC$\pi _{i}>r^{C}$and who earns a return ofri<rC$r_{i}<r^{C}$in equilibrium. If the distribution of firms is sufficiently dense, then there exists an unfinanced firmj$j$with profitsπj>ri$\pi _{j}>r_{i}$such that if the values-aligned social investori$i$were to deviate and offer firmj$j$financing at costπj$\pi _{j}$, both total social welfare and investori$i$’s financial return would increase.

In the proof of Proposition2(see SectionII.C.1of theInternet Appendix), we formalize the notion that the distribution of entrepreneurs issufficiently dense. Intuitively, this argument guarantees that for any combination ofπ$\pi$andw$w$, there exists an entrepreneur with profitsπi$\pi _{i}$nearπ$\pi$and social valuewi$w_{i}$nearw$w$. Proposition2can be understood with reference to Figure3. Consider any values-aligned social investori$i$who supports firmi$i$with profitsπi≥rC$\pi _{i}\ge r^{C}$and who earns financial returnri<rC$r_{i}<r^{C}$(this holds generically for all values-aligned investors who support firms withπi≥rC$\pi _{i}\ge r^{C}$). These investors support the firms highlighted in the three darker shades of blue. Now consider among the set of unfinanced firms some firmj$j$with profitsπj>ri$\pi _{j}>r_{i}$and with social valuewj>0$w_{j}>0$. This firm is guaranteed to exist by the assumption that the distribution of firms is dense. One such firm is highlighted in green.
The contribution to social welfare of the equilibrium investment for investori$i$is zero, regardless of the social valuewi$w_{i}$of firmi$i$, as investori$i$is merely displacing commercial investment. Firmj$j$creates less social value than any firm in the blue region of the diagram, but by reallocating investori$i$’s capital to firmj$j$’s social welfare increases, as firmj$j$was previously unfinanced.
Further, by offering firmj$j$a cost of capitalrj=πj$r_{j}=\pi _{j}$, investori$i$can earn a higher financial return. As with social value, firmj$j$earns lower profits than any firm in the blue region of the diagram. But becauseπj>ri$\pi _{j}>r_{i}$, the subsidy required to make firmj$j$profitable is smaller than the financial concession (relative to the commercial rate of return) that investori$i$made to own firmi$i$. Critically, values-aligned social investors compete down the price of capital of firms with large contributions to social value even when these firms could have attracted commercial financing. The financial compromise made by values-aligned investors to support such firms results in a transfer of wealth to the entrepreneur rather than expanding the pool of socially valuable firms. In contrast, the financial compromise made to support a firm that could not attract commercial financing goes entirely to expanding the pool of socially valuable firms rather than to a transfer of rents to entrepreneurs whose projects would have been feasible anyway.
We note that the proof of Proposition2relies on allowing the deviating values-aligned investor to extract the full profit of the previously unfinanced firm highlighted in green in Figure3. However, this feature of the model is not necessary for the result. The critical ingredient is that values-aligned investors bid up the price of some firms in a way that does not contribute to aggregate social value (i.e., by competing up the price of socially valuable projects that would have occurred even at the commercial cost of capital). So long as a portion of that financial concession can be reallocated in a way thatdoescontribute to social value (e.g., by donating it to charity), then the deviating values-aligned investor can reduce the total financial concession and allocate the remainder in a way that creates social value, increasing both impact and financial return.
Finally, we note that while there exist opportunities for values-aligned investors to increase their financial return and social impact, these opportunities do not increase the utility of values-aligned social investors (who place intrinsic value on the social value of the company they finance). That is, for investors who truly hold values-aligned preferences, the deviation that we analyzed above would not be attractive. Nevertheless, in SectionIV, we provide evidence that some investors who employ values-aligned strategies are willing to deviate to investments that increase their financial return and impact (and, parallel to the discussion above, that have lower social value than their original investments). Proposition2may therefore offer useful guidance for increasing the impact of social investing.
How should one judge the contribution to social welfare of a particular entrepreneur, sometimes referred to as enterprise impact (Brest, Gilson, and Wolfson (2019))? On first pass, it might seem natural forwi$w_{i}$to be the measure of enterprise impact. We argue, however, that a firm's enterprise impact should also account for the social value of the capital it employs.
For simplicity, in this section, we assume that all social investors have homogeneous altruism parameterθ$\theta$. LetWSV,SI$W\left(S_{V},S_{I}\right)$be the total social value created in equilibrium given masses of values-aligned investorsSV$S_{V}$and impact-aligned investorsSI$S_{I}$. Denote byνSV$\nu _{S_{V}}$the increase in social value corresponding to adding one additional values-aligned social investor, and byνSI$\text{$\nu _{S_{I}}$}$the increase in social value corresponding to adding one additional impact-aligned social investor. The social value of commercial capital,νC$\nu _{C}$, is normalized to zero. It is straightforward to show thatνC<νSV<νSI$\nu _{C}<\nu _{S_{V}}<\nu _{S_{I}}$.
We define the enterprise impact of firmi$i$to beei≡wi−νi$e_{i}\equiv w_{i}-\nu _{i}$, whereνi$\nu _{i}$is the social value of capital attributable to the investor who supports entrepreneuri$i$in equilibrium. We define the enterprise impact for firms that do not receive financing to be zero. Enterprise impactei$e_{i}$corresponds to the change in total social value created in equilibrium from adding firmi$i$to the market.
This definition of enterprise impact might have practical value for socially motivated investors who aim to quantify the social value of a particular enterprise. Frontier efforts in the impact investing industry often attempt to account for both the social value created by the enterprise and the amount of capital employed by the enterprise, such as in the impact multiple of money method (Addy et al. (2019)). Our analysis shows that it is also critical to account for the composition of social capital versus commercial capital raised by an enterprise in judging its impact.
This definition of enterprise impact also highlights an alignment between a firm's enterprise impact and profitability. Firms can increase their enterprise impact by increasing their profitabilityeven holding fixed their social valuewi$w_{i}$. Increasing the profitability of the firm makes it more likely to attract commercial capital, freeing up capital that is willing to accept lower returns to fund higher social-value endeavors. In particular, we have the following result.
Proposition 3.Suppose firmi$i$attracts financing from an impact-aligned social investor in equilibrium. Increasing its profitsπi$\pi _{i}$while holding fixed its social valuewi$w_{i}$weakly increases its enterprise impactei$e_{i}$and total social value created in equilibrium.

Importantly, this result is not driven by assuming that a firm's profitability and its social value are correlated. Instead, this result is driven by the observation that once a firm becomes profitable enough to attract commercial financing, impact-aligned social investors will step aside, freeing up their capital to support another socially valuable firm. Therefore, more profitable firms use less socially valuable capital and have higher enterprise impact. Note that this phenomenon does not hold for firms supported by values-aligned social investors, as values-aligned social investors pay no regard to whether a firm could attract commercial capital in their absence.
article.III. Incomplete Information:
A key challenge in socially motivated investing is evaluating how a given investment opportunity satisfies the social dimension of an investor's preferences. The results of our model suggest that such assessments may be particularly difficult for investors who have preferences for achieving impact. Both values- and impact-aligned strategies require knowing the social value of the set of available investments. But impact-aligned strategies also require knowing whether other investors, with their own social preferences, would provide financing for these investment opportunities. In more realistic informational environments, characterized by uncertainty about whether a firm could attract capital from less socially minded investors, how can impact-aligned investors operationalize their preference for impact? And, which dimensions of the informational environment are most important to be able to create social impact as an investor? To shed light on these questions, we explore a version of our model with informational frictions that prevent investors from perfectly determining both the social value of a project and the investor's own pivotally in realizing that social value through their potential investment.
We modify our baseline model to incorporate incomplete information as follows. Instead of a firm having publicly observable profitsπi∈R+$\pi _{i}\in \mathbb {R}^{+}$and social valuewi∈R$w_{i}\in \mathbb {R}$, these quantities now represent public signals ofexpectedprofits and social value, respectively. Entrepreneurs have private information about their true type,πi,wi∈πL,πH×wL,wH$\left(\pi ^{i},w^{i}\right)\in \left\lbrace \pi ^{L},\pi ^{H}\right\rbrace \times \left\lbrace w^{L},w^{H}\right\rbrace$, withπH>rC>πL$\pi ^{H}>r^{C}>\pi ^{L}$andwH>wL$w^{H}>w^{L}$. Public signals thus map into probabilities of being high-profit and high social-value types. Without loss of generality, we normalizewL=πL=0$w^{L}=\pi ^{L}=0$andwH=πH=1$w^{H}=\pi ^{H}=1$and thus interpretπi$\pi _{i}$andwi$w_{i}$as the probabilities of firmi$i$being a high-profit type and a high social-value type. We assume that for all firmsi$i$,πi∈0,1$\pi _{i}\in \left(0,1\right)$, so that every firm has a positive probability of being a high-profit type or low-profit type. As in SectionI, we make no assumption about the joint distribution ofπi,wi$\left(\pi _{i},w_{i}\right)$, although we assume that for a given observable typeπi,wi$\left(\pi _{i},w_{i}\right)$, realizations ofπi$\pi ^{i}$andwi$w^{i}$are independent. For simplicity, we assume that all social investors have uniform altruismθ$\theta$.
We now turn to characterizing the investment strategies of impact-aligned investors in the incomplete information model. As with the model with complete information, impact-aligned social investors aim to support firms that not only have high social value but also are unlikely to attract commercial financing. In the absence of impact-aligned social capital, each firmi$i$will receive financing if and only ifπi=πH$\pi ^{i}=\pi ^{H}$. Recall that while the true profit type is private information, we assume it is not feasible for firms to obtain capital at ratesr>πi$r>\pi ^{i}$.
Since we assumeπH>rC>πL$\pi ^{H}>r^{C}>\pi ^{L}$, no low-profit type can obtain commercial financing, while all high-profit types are able to access commercial financing. Therefore, the probability that an impact-aligned social investor is pivotal in financing a given firmi$i$is1−πi$1-\pi _{i}$, the probability the firm is the low-profit type. Further, notice that whenever impact-aligned investors offer financing, they do so at rateri=πL$r_{i}=\pi ^{L}$. This is because offering financing at higher cost could only be accepted by high-profit–type firms, which would generate no social value, and such firms would only accept offers ofr≤rC$r\le r^{C}$, which for the investor is dominated by putting their funds in the outside commercial capital market and earningrC$r^{C}$.
Defineu¯S$\bar{u}^{S}$such thati∈E:πL+θwi1−πi≥u¯S=S$\left|\left\lbrace i\in E:\pi ^{L}+\theta w_{i}\left(1-\pi _{i}\right)\ge \bar{u}^{S}\right\rbrace \right|=\left|S\right|$. Social capital is scarce ifu¯S>rC$\bar{u}^{S}>r^{C}$, the natural extension of Assumption1. This leads to the following characterization of the firms financed by social investors.
Proposition 4.Assume that social capital is scarce,u¯S>rC$\bar{u}^{S}>r^{C}$. Then impact-aligned social investors finance all projects withπi,wi$\left(\pi _{i},w_{i}\right)$satisfying


In line with Proposition4, the left panel of Figure4depicts the equilibrium allocation of social capital under incomplete information. Thicker horizontal bars indicate higher likelihood of commercial financing. Proposition4generates an immediate corollary.
Corollary 1.Under incomplete information, impact-aligned investors finance high-profit, high social-value firms. That is, for anyw$w$, there is aπ¯w$\bar{\pi }\left(w\right)$such that impact-aligned investors will finance any firm withwi=w$w_{i}=w$andπi≤π¯w$\pi _{i}\le \bar{\pi }\left(w\right)$, withπ¯w$\bar{\pi }\left(w\right)$increasing inw$w$.

Corollary1shows that when there is incomplete information about which firms are commercially viable, the behavior of impact-aligned social investors resembles that of values-aligned social investors in the model with complete information. The right panel of Figure4demonstrates this graphically. It shows both how impact-aligned investors allocate capital under incomplete information, and, from Figure3, the investment opportunities region in which values-aligned investors are the exclusive providers of capital under complete information about fundamentals. Under incomplete information, impact-aligned investors are willing to finance some firms with high social value and high expected profits, as there remains a chance that some of these firms are not commercially viable. Therefore, the observation that, in practice, many social investors finance projects that deliver both high profit and high social value does not necessarily imply that these investors are employing values-aligned strategies. Rather, their behavior may be consistent with the efficient creation of impact in the face of uncertainty about their own pivotality.
A key takeaway from the analysis above is that incomplete information about profitability affects investors' ability to assess whether their investment would be pivotal and thus generate social value. This result occurs because incomplete information about profitability leads to uncertainty about which investments would be able to attract commercial capital. Other forms of incomplete information could also affect an impact-aligned investor's assessment of how likely they are to be pivotal, but through other channels. For example, incomplete information about the preferences and strategies of other investors, how much social capital is available, or the distribution of potential investment opportunities can affect inference on which opportunities could attract other social investors. We conjecture that in all of these cases, impact-aligned investors will form strategies that weigh expected returns and expected impact, with the latter accounting for how incomplete information affects the equilibrium probability that a given investment would be pivotal. We leave for future research a more detailed characterization of how these various forms of incomplete information affect investment strategies and equilibrium capital allocation.
article.IV. Empirical Evidence and Practical Imp:
Above we analyze a model of the behavior of investors with different types of social preferences. How prevalent are impact- and values-aligned investing preferences in practice? Do all investors correctly operationalize their preferences through their investment strategies, or do some investors who have a preference for impact mistakenly follow values-aligned strategies? In this section, we analyze novel survey data to shed light on these questions.
Two primary findings emerge from our laboratory setting. First, we find significant heterogeneity in investors' preferences. We identify three classes of social investors based on their choices among investment opportunities: those whose choices match the behavior of impact-aligned investors in our model, those whose choices match values-alignment, and those whose choices match commercial investors. Second, we find that social preferences as revealed by investment strategies do not perfectly match stated preferences a sizable fraction of participants who adopt values-aligned investment strategies in our survey state preferences for generating impact. Further, in line with Proposition2, among those who make choices consistent with values-aligned preferences, half are willing to change their choices when they are shown that doing so would increase social impact and improve their financial returns. This suggests the findings of our paper are potentially important—a large amount of socially-motivated investing takes the form of values-aligned strategies, yet these investors may in fact care primarily about creating social impact.
Our analysis in this section builds on the work of Bonnefon et al. (2023), who elicit willingness-to-pay for social attributes of investment opportunities and employ charitable donations as a proxy for social value. Our analysis differs in three key respects. First, we explicitly account for the possibility of heterogeneity in social investing preferences. Second, our survey instrument elicits preferences from pairwise choices, while Bonnefon et al. (2023) elicit willingness-to-pay for individual stocks. These differences could explain our finding that part of the population demonstrates significant willingness to pay for impact, whereas Bonnefon et al. (2023) do not find statistically significant evidence that the representative social investor is willing to pay to achieve social impact. Third, we demonstrate that some of the investors who follow values-aligned strategies may in fact have impact-aligned preferences.
We employ a laboratory-style elicitation exercise to infer the preferences of investors. In November 2023, we recruited 400 participants on the Prolific online survey and experiment platform. We screened for respondents that were based in the United States and have financial investments in the stock market.
In the experiment, we asked participants to indicate in a series of scenarios,s=1,…,S$s=1,\ldots,S$, which of two potential stocksk$k$andk′$k^{\prime }$they found more appealing. The attributes of each stockk$k$are its financial return,rk∈$3.00,$3.50,$4.00,$4.50$r_{k}\in \left\lbrace \$3.00,\$3.50,\$4.00,\$4.50\right\rbrace $, its social value operationalized as the donation the given company makes to the charity Feeding America,wk∈$0.50,$1.00,$1.50,$2.00$w_{k}\in \left\lbrace \$0.50,\$1.00,\$1.50,\$2.00\right\rbrace $, and whether the investor's purchase of the stock is pivotal to generating the company's charitable donation,pk∈0,1$p_{k}\in \left\lbrace 0,1\right\rbrace $. The cost of investing in a stock is normalized to zero, so an investor who chooses a stock with returnr$r$receives a payout of $r$r$. Figure5shows one of the choice decisions in our experiment.
The fact that realizing social value can be a function of the investment decision captures the notion of investor impact. In the survey introduction, we use an example scenario to explain in detail how this works in our experimental setting. We explain to participants that if neither company's charitable donation depends on the participant's investment, their choice has no effect on the total charitable donation. If both companies' donations depend on the participant's investment, the only charitable donation will come from the company whose stock they purchase. If only one of the company's donations depends on the participant's investment, the participant increases the amount of the charitable donation only by choosing that company's stock. For example, in the question shown in Figure5, choosing to invest in Company A results in a $1.00 donation to charity. However, because Company A will donate regardless of the investment decision, choosing to invest in Company B results in a $1.50 donation to charity, even though Company B itself donates only $0.50 to charity.
Each question is designed so that the answer reveals something about the preferences of the participant. For example, consider the question shown in Figure5. Specifically, choosing Company A stock is consistent with values-aligned preferences—it offers a lower financial return but a higher social value than the alternative option. Choosing Company A stock reveals that the participant was willing to incur $0.50 lower financial return and $0.50 lower social impact in order to obtain $0.50 higher social value. In our sample, 23% of respondents chose company A under this scenario. By presenting investors with a series of choices between two assets with distinct characteristics, we can estimate the strength of their preferences for values alignment and impact alignment.
Of the 14 investment scenarios, six were structured to also explore the possibility that investors may have impact-aligned preferences but may be incorrectly operationalizing them through values-aligned strategies. As described in more detail below, depending on their responses to these questions, participants may be asked follow-up questions about their choice after the main survey.
Our first results correspond to the relative strength of values- and impact-aligned preferences. Here our focus is on identifying the degree to which investors are heterogeneous in their social preferences, and the degree to which this heterogeneity maps to the three types of preferences we study in our model: purely financially motivated, values-aligned preferences (placing monetary value onw$w$but not on pivotally), and impact-aligned preferences (placing value onw$w$only in cases in which the investment is pivotal).
Consistent with our theory, we specifyC=3$C=3$latent classes. TableIreports the results of this exercise. The three columns specify the estimates of willingness-to-pay for impact and values for each of the three latent classes, as well as their estimated class shares.10
Strikingly, the estimation results reveal preference heterogeneity that closely corresponds to the different types of investors in our theoretical model. “Class 1” has relatively little willingness to pay for either impact or values—investors correspond to the commercial investors in our framework. The other classes have stronger social investing preferences. “Class 2” has a strong preference for values alignment. On average, investors of this type are willing to forgo $0.72 of financial return in exchange for a $1 increase in values alignment of their investment decision. This group has much lower preference for impact. “Class 3” is consistent with impact-aligned investors in our model. Investors of this type have a strong willingness to pay to achieve social impact, with a willingness to pay for impact of $0.90 but no willingness to pay for values alignment.
A potential concern with this analysis is that it does not capture true heterogeneity in individual preferences, but rather these results arise mechanically or spuriously. There are several reasons to reject this concern. First, we can show that the estimation captures individual-level rather than choice-level preference heterogeneity. Estimation of the model above also allows us to compute respondent-specific posterior probabilities of class membership implied by their choices in the experiment. Posterior probabilities of modal class membership are very high, 95% of respondents have a modal class membership probability of over 80%. The preference heterogeneity we document is therefore arising across, rather than within, individual participants in the experiment.11Second, simulated choice data do not generate these patterns of results. The presence of multiple clusters is consistently rejected when response data are generated from homogeneous preferences. Taken together, these results suggest that the analysis above provides compelling evidence that both impact alignment and values alignment capture the ways people think about socially motivated investment.
One fundamental message of this paper is that for investors to most efficiently have social impact, they need to prioritize making investments that could not attract less altruistic capital. This message would be especially important if investors who want to have an impact are mistakenly adopting values-aligned strategies. Our survey exercise generates two pieces of evidence suggesting that some investors who adopt values-aligned strategies are indeed making a mistake.
First, we compare the stated social preferences of investors in our survey to the preferences revealed by their choices in our investment scenarios. We find a sizable number of investors who adopt values-aligned investment strategies, but self-report as having impact-aligned preferences. To capture self-reported social preferences, the survey described values-aligned and impact-aligned investing objectives and asked which, if any, the participant identified most with.12Of the 389 respondents in our sample, 125 identified as impact-aligned investors, 144 identified as values-aligned investors, and 120 stated they did not incorporate social considerations into investing decisions. To capture revealed preferences, we use the respondent's modal posterior class membership from the model estimated in the previous section. Among the participants identified by their choice data as employing values-aligned strategies, 30% self-reported as having impact-aligned preferences.
Second, as mentioned above, of the 14 investment scenarios, six were structured to also explore the possibility that some investors who adopt values-aligned strategies are doing so mistakenly. In each of these six scenarios, one choice is only consistent with values-aligned preferences: It requires giving up both (weakly) higher financial returns and strictly higher social impact to attain higher values alignment. At the end of the survey, we ask follow-up questions of participants that picked the low-return/high-value stock in at least one of the scenarios. In the follow-up questions, in the spirit of Proposition2, we point out that the participant could have earned at least as much money but generated more social impact by picking the other stock. We then ask if they would like to revise their answer. See SectionIIIof theInternet Appendixfor the exact language of the follow-up question.
Note that if the investors' initial decisions reflected their true preferences, no participant would revise their decision when presented the opportunity to do so. In contrast, if participants truly have a preference for creating impact and, to operationalize this preference, were applying a heuristic of investing in companies with higher social value, then we should expect some of these participants to change their behavior when they are informed that a different choice would provide higher social impact and generate a higher financial return.
In our data, 59% of participants chose the values-aligned option in at least one of these six scenarios and therefore offered an opportunity to revise their decision. Of those participants, 109 out of 231 revised their choice each time they were given an opportunity to do so. These results show that nearly half of the participants whose choices indicated values-aligned preferences may in fact have a preference for impact that is not reflected in their initial investment decisions. This finding supports a normative interpretation of our results—some investors that implement values-aligned strategies in practice might be doing so in error, and following the impact-aligned investment strategies in our model could improve their welfare and social impact.
article.V. Conclusion:
This paper provides a new framework to understand how values-based investing generates social impact in an environment in which social and commercial investors compete. We analyze a model in which investors influence social outcomes through their asset allocation. We show that equilibrium asset allocation varies in important ways with how precisely social investors think about social value creation.
Investors following values-aligned investment strategies, which closely resemble the construction of conventional ESG and emissions reduction portfolios, have limited impact because they simply displace commercial investors who do not care about social value creation but would have supported some socially valuable companies anyway. Further, because values-aligned investors place intrinsic value on owning socially valuable firms, they compete to own firms pushing up the price of firms that could have been financed by commercial investors. From the perspective of generating impact, we show that this financial concession is inefficient. We identify an alternative investment approach that only makes financial concessions to subsidize firms that would not be viable at the commercial cost of capital. This approach generates greater impact and higher financial returns.
We demonstrate two further implications of the view that impact can arise from not displacing investors who care less about social value. First, we show that when there is heterogeneity in the altruism of impact-aligned investors, the equilibrium involves negative assortative matching high-altruism investors finance lower social-value projects so as to avoid displacing lower altruism investors from investing in high social-value projects. Second, from a firm's perspective, we show that making a firm more profitable can also make it more impactful. Social investors who care about their financial return and their contribution to social value seek not to invest in firms that could have attracted commercial capital. Taking a firm that would have been financed by an impact-aligned investor and making it more profitable allows it to use less socially valuable capital and thereby free up the impact-aligned capital to support a new firm.
We next consider more realistic informational environments in which impact-aligned investors cannot perfectly infer when their investment in a firm would be pivotal and have impact. In particular, under incomplete information about firm profitability, impact-aligned strategies resemble values-aligned strategies in a complete-information environment. More generally, because incomplete information affects impact-aligned investors' ability to identify investment opportunities that would not have been funded by others, it limits the ability of these investors to generate impact. This result suggests that impact-aligned investors should focus on measuring and generating improved information about not only the social value of the investment opportunity, but also its ability to attract capital from other investors. How to do so optimally represents an interesting question for future research.
Finally, the practical importance of our findings hinges on the degree to which social investors have a preference for impact creation and the degree to which some social investors with a preference for impact creation incorrectly operationalize their preference through values-aligned strategies. We provide novel empirical evidence documenting that a sizable fraction of investors have a preference for impact alignment and that many of these investors do indeed incorrectly operationalize that preference. These empirical findings underscore the relevance of our theory.
Editors: Antoinette Schoar, Urban Jermann, Leonid Kogan, Jonathan Lewellen, and Thomas Philippon
